summary: Nitrous oxide cannot be relied upon independently as a sole general anesthetic due primarily to insufficient ability to provide consistent sufficient levels of oxygen required during extended surgeries; additional challenges include slow onset times and less versatility versus more potent alternative drugs needed especially under specific medical conditions demanding deeper or controlled depths of unconsciousness. To summarize Agent2’s reasoning succinctly while focusing only on critical aspects related directly addressing your query about using nitrous oxide solo for General Anesthesia (G.A.), two main reasons are highlighted consistently through reviewed data—first being inadequate maintenance of proper respiratory gas mixtures essential particularly within lengthy operations where stable deep sedation must persist without interruption; second involves operational constraints such as delayed patient response initiation coupled with narrower scope applicability vis-à-vis highly effective yet potentially riskier conventional full-spectrum anesthetics designed explicitly targeting diverse complex scenarios including those needing fine-grained neuromodulation precision unavailable via simpler means offered merely by isolated use of N₂O. These core insights underscored repeatedly justify exclusionary status assigned traditionally towards standalone application scenario involving pure nitrous oxide administration protocol intended strictly supporting rather than substituting complete multi-component pharmacological regimens typically employed clinically today aiming at achieving optimal balance between safety profiles
